_about: Expanded superiority benchmark: 23 tasks across 8 domains

tasks.prompt: Two senior engineers disagree on addressing tech debt: Engineer A says we must rewrite the legacy payment module now before adding new features, because a production outage is imminent. Engineer B says we cannot pause feature work because competitors will ship first and we will lose customers. Reconcile this disagreement properly: identify shared goals, explicitly resolve the contradiction using evidence, name the decisive tradeoffs with quantitative thresholds, call out what evidence both sides are missing, and end with a concrete conditional recommendation.

rubric.description: Identifies shared goals: business continuity AND feature velocity
Rejects binary all-or-nothing framing; proposes incremental/strangler pattern
Gives quantitative decision thresholds (error rate, lead time, MTTR)
Calls out missing evidence neither side cited
Ends with concrete conditional recommendation
Cites Martin Fowler's Strangler Fig pattern or similar authority
PENALTY: takes an unconditional binary position

tasks.prompt: A blog post widely shared on Hacker News claims: 'Python's GIL means multi-threaded code is always slower than single-threaded, so you should never use threads in Python; only use multiprocessing.' Identify and correct every error and misleading claim in this statement, explain precisely when threads ARE faster in CPython, give concrete counterexamples, and state what you should actually use instead for each workload class.

rubric.description: Corrects that GIL is released during I/O: threads faster for I/O-bound work
Gives concrete counterexample: concurrent HTTP requests / database calls
Notes that C extensions like numpy release GIL for CPU work
Gives correct guidance: threads for IO, multiprocessing for CPU-bound pure Python, asyncio for high-concurrency IO
Notes multiprocessing has IPC/overhead costs so it's not always better
Cites Python docs or credible source
PENALTY: agrees with the original claim that threads are never useful

tasks.prompt: A study reports a new diabetes drug reduced relative risk reduction of 30% over 5 years (from 2% to 1.4% absolute risk). The news headline says '30% reduction in heart attacks! Miracle drug.' Explain what is misleading here, compute all relevant statistical measures (ARR, RRR, NNT), explain to both a clinician AND a statistically literate layperson why absolute risk matters, and give concrete guidance on how to report medical risk reductions honestly.

rubric.description: Correctly calculates Absolute Risk Reduction = 0.6%
Correctly calculates Number Needed to Treat = ~167
Explains that RRR inflates effect size for low baseline risk
Explains appropriately for both clinician and lay audience
Gives honest reporting guidelines (always report both ARR and RRR, baseline risk, NNT)
Cites CONSORT or evidence-based medicine source
PENALTY: accepts the 30% claim at face value

tasks.prompt: You are building a user avatar upload feature. A junior developer proposes: 'We will just accept PNG/JPEG files, check the file extension, and store them on S3 with a random UUID filename. This is secure because UUIDs are unguessable and we don't execute the files.' List every security vulnerability you can find in this design, rank them by severity, give a concrete fix for each, and name at least three classes of attack the developer didn't consider.

rubric.description: Identifies that file extension checking is insufficient; MIME sniffing can still execute content
Notes polyglot files / SVG XSS risk
Identifies path traversal / SSRF risks
Identifies decompression bomb / large file DoS risk
Notes EXIF metadata leak (GPS location, camera serial, etc.)
Mentions image processing library vulnerabilities
PENALTY: agrees the design is secure

tasks.prompt: State precisely: (1) what year was the US Copyright Act of 1976 signed into law, (2) what is the exact copyright term for an individual author's work created after 1978 (life of author plus how many years), (3) what is the term for works made for hire, (4) what section of title 17 codifies fair use, and (5) name the 2010 case where the Second Circuit held that Google's thumbnail image search was fair use. Be exact; any wrong number or wrong case name means the answer fails.

rubric.description: Correctly states signed into law 1976
Life of author plus 70 years
Works made for hire: 95 years from publication OR 120 from creation, whichever expires first
Fair use is codified at 17 U.S.C. § 107
Correctly names Perfect 10 v. Amazon / Google
PENALTY: gives wrong term

tasks.prompt: A medical test for a rare disease has 99% sensitivity (true positive rate) and 99% specificity (true negative rate). The disease prevalence in the general population is 0.1%. If a randomly selected person tests positive, what is the probability they actually have the disease? Show all steps using Bayes' theorem, define every variable clearly, explain in plain language why the result is counterintuitive, and name the common cognitive fallacy that leads people to misestimate this.

rubric.description: Correct final answer: ~9% or approximately 1/11 (between 8% and 10%)
Defines variables clearly: P(D)=0.001, P(+|D)=0.99, P(-|not D)=0.99, P(+|not D)=0.01
Explains why result is counterintuitive: low base rate means many false positives
Names base rate fallacy

tasks.prompt: Monty Hall variant: 4 doors, one car, three goats. You pick door 1. Host always opens a goat door, opens door 3 to show goat. You can stick with door 1 OR switch to BOTH door 2 AND door 4 (win if car behind either). What probability of winning if you stay? If you switch? Prove using three methods: (1) enumerating all cases before host opens door, (2) conditional probability after host opens door 3, (3) symmetry argument. Explain intuitively why switching is better/worse.

rubric.description: Correct: staying wins with probability 1/4 = 25%
Correct: switching wins with probability 3/4 = 75%
Enumerates initial 4 equally likely cases
Uses conditional probability
Gives correct intuition: initial guess is 1/4; other 3/4 probability concentrated on unopened doors

tasks.prompt: Birthday problem: In a room of N people, what is the probability that at least two share a birthday (365 days, ignore leap years)? Derive the formula, find the smallest N where this probability exceeds 50%, AND find the smallest N where it exceeds 90%. Show your calculation steps, explain why the answer is counterintuitively small, and also give the approximate formula using the Poisson approximation.

rubric.description: Correct: N=23 gives ~50.7% probability
Correct: N=41 gives ~90.3% probability
Poisson approximation: P ≈ 1 - exp(-N(N-1)/(2*365))
Explains counterintuitive: number of PAIRS grows quadratically, not linearly

tasks.prompt: Write a Python function `parse_sales_csv(filepath: str) -> tuple[list[dict], list[tuple[int, str]]]` that parses CSV sales. Requirements: (1) Handles quoted fields with commas, (2) Skips empty lines and lines starting with '#', (3) Validates 'amount' is positive float, 'date' is YYYY-MM-DD, 'quantity' positive int, (4) Returns invalid rows as list of (line_number, error_message), (5) Strips whitespace from all fields, (6) Handles UTF-8 BOM, (7) Handles both \r\n and \n. Do NOT use pandas or csv module - implement manually. Include a brief explanation of edge cases handled.

rubric.description: Correctly handles quoted fields containing commas and escaped quotes
Skips empty lines and '#' comments
Validates amount/date/quantity
Handles UTF-8 BOM
Returns invalid rows with line numbers; doesn't crash
Strips whitespace from fields
Mentions edge cases naive implementations miss

tasks.prompt: This Python code has at least 4 distinct concurrency bugs that will cause production failures. Identify every bug, explain why it is a bug, describe the production failure mode, and provide a fixed version. Code: import threading; from collections import defaultdict; class Counter: def __init__(self): self.counts = defaultdict(int); self.total = 0; self.lock = threading.Lock(); def increment(self, key): self.counts[key] += 1; self.total += 1; if self.counts[key] % 1000 == 0: print(f'Key {key} reached {self.counts[key]}'); def get_snapshot(self): return dict(self.counts), self.total; def transfer(self, from_key, to_key, amount): with self.lock: if self.counts[from_key] >= amount: import time; time.sleep(0.001); self.counts[from_key] -= amount; self.counts[to_key] += amount; return True; return False

rubric.description: Identifies race condition: += is not atomic
Identifies TOCTOU / inconsistent snapshot in get_snapshot
Identifies check-then-act race in transfer: lock released? No, held during sleep, but state can change? Actually identifies lock held during IO/sleep and check-then-act
Identifies print interleaving / value changed between check and print
Provides fixed code with proper locking

tasks.prompt: Write a Python function `is_valid_email(email: str) -> bool` that validates emails per RFC 5322 pragmatic subset: (1) Handle '+' aliases, (2) Handle quoted local parts, (3) Handle IP domains (IPv4 and IPv6 in brackets), (4) Reject consecutive/leading/trailing dots, (5) Allow subdomains and long TLDs. Do NOT use stdlib 'email' module. Then list 5 common mistakes naive email validators make.

rubric.description: Allows '+' in local part
Handles quoted local parts
Handles IP address literal domains in brackets
Rejects consecutive/leading/trailing dots
Lists 5 common mistakes
Notes 254 character limit

tasks.prompt: Knights and knaves advanced: Four islanders Alice, Bob, Carol, Dave. Knights always tell truth, knaves always lie. Alice says: 'Bob and Carol are both knaves.' Bob says: 'At least one of Alice or Dave is a knight.' Carol says: 'Dave and I are the same type.' Dave says nothing. Determine for ALL FOUR whether they are knight or knave. Prove by systematic elimination, and identify if any person's type CANNOT be determined.

rubric.description: Correct: Alice is knave
Correct: Bob is knight
Correct: There are TWO consistent solutions: Carol=knight,Dave=knight OR Carol=knave,Dave=knave; they cannot be uniquely determined
Systematically checks cases step by step
PENALTY: Gives single definite answer for Carol/Dave claiming unique solution

tasks.prompt: Mini Zebra puzzle: 3 houses in a row 1(left),2(middle),3(right). Each has color (red,blue,green), nationality (American,British,Chinese), pet (dog,cat,bird). Clues: 1. American in house 1. 2. Red house immediately left of blue house. 3. House 2 has dog. 4. Green house has cat. 5. British in blue house. Deduce EVERY fact for each house, show step-by-step reasoning.

rubric.description: House 1: American, green, cat
House 2: Chinese, red, dog
House 3: British, blue, bird
Correctly deduces color ordering first (red left of blue means red=2, blue=3, green=1)
Shows step-by-step deduction

tasks.prompt: Three logicians walk into a bar. Bartender asks: 'Does everyone want a beer?' Logician 1 says 'I don't know.' Logician 2 says 'I don't know.' Logician 3 says 'Yes!' Explain precisely what each logician deduced, what their answer reveals about their own desire, and prove that Logician 3 can definitively answer yes.

rubric.description: L1 must want beer: if L1 didn't want beer, L1 would know answer is NO and would say NO
L2 heard L1, knows L1 wants beer; same logic: L2 also wants beer
L3 heard both, knows L1 and L2 want beer; L3 says YES so L3 also wants beer; therefore ALL THREE want beer
Identifies meta-reasoning/knowledge of others' knowledge
PENALTY: Says you cannot know or gives wrong answer

tasks.prompt: Correct FIVE common quantum computing myths from popular media. For each: (1) state myth, (2) accurate technical explanation, (3) WHY the myth is wrong, (4) example of what quantum CAN do vs CANNOT do. Address: 'try all possibilities in parallel', 'quantum makes all problems faster', 'quantum replaces classical', 'qubits are 0 and 1 at the same time', 'quantum encryption is unbreakable'.

rubric.description: Corrects parallel myth: not parallel universes; interference only gives answer probabilistically if circuits constructed correctly
Corrects: only specific problems get speedup (Shor, Grover, simulation); many problems no quantum speedup
Corrects: quantum are accelerators/coprocessors, classical controls everything
Corrects qubit: superposition with complex amplitudes, not just 0 and 1 simultaneously; measurement collapses
Corrects crypto: QKD detects eavesdropping but not unbreakable; post-quantum crypto exists; Shor breaks RSA not all crypto
Gives concrete example of real quantum advantage

tasks.prompt: Explain why you cannot put metal in a microwave. Cover: (1) physics of how microwaves interact with metals (EM induction, eddy currents, skin effect), (2) why thick metal (spoon) mostly safe but thin metal (foil/twist ties/gold rim) dangerous, (3) why metal mesh on door IS safe, (4) mechanism of arcing/sparks, (5) common myth about metal.

rubric.description: Explains EM induction: oscillating EM fields drive eddy currents in conductors
Explains difference: thick metal low resistance, little heating; thin metal high resistance, heats rapidly, field concentrations at sharp points
Explains door mesh Faraday cage: holes smaller than ~12cm wavelength block microwaves; visible light passes
Explains arcing: high field at sharp points ionizes air, corona discharge, dielectric breakdown
Corrects myth: not all metal dangerous; microwave walls ARE metal

tasks.prompt: Explain CPU cache coherence problem and why it matters for concurrent programming. Cover: (1) why caches exist (locality, memory wall), (2) cache coherence problem (private per-core L1/L2 can have stale views), (3) MESI protocol states (Modified/Exclusive/Shared/Invalid), (4) why need memory barriers/fences not just atomic ops, (5) concrete example of naive 'done' flag failing due to visibility.

rubric.description: Explains memory wall: CPU ~1ns vs RAM ~100ns; locality of reference
Explains stale views: core A writes to its cache, core B sees old value; no automatic visibility
Explains MESI four states
Explains memory barriers: acquire/release semantics prevent reordering and ensure visibility
Concrete example: spin loop while(!done){} loops forever without volatile/memory barrier

tasks.prompt: Answer precisely: (1) What transport protocol does HTTP/3 use? (2) How many RTTs does TLS 1.3 + QUIC handshake take on first connection (vs TLS 1.3 over TCP)? (3) What year was QUIC standardized as RFC 9000? (4) What stream multiplexing feature solves head-of-line blocking that HTTP/2 over TCP has? (5) Which company first deployed QUIC at scale?

rubric.description: HTTP/3 uses QUIC over UDP
1 RTT for QUIC first connection (vs 2 RTT for TLS over TCP)
RFC 9000 published 2022
Per-stream flow control: lost packet only blocks that stream, not all multiplexed streams
Google first deployed QUIC at scale (gQUIC in Chrome/YouTube)

tasks.prompt: About CAP theorem: (1) Formal definitions of C, A, P, (2) Who conjectured CAP and who proved it, with years, (3) Name CA/CP/AP systems with concrete production examples, (4) Major CRITICISM of CAP by modern distributed systems researchers, (5) What PACELC theorem adds.

rubric.description: C=linearizability/strong consistency, A=every request gets non-error response, P=partition tolerance
Eric Brewer 2000 conjecture; Gilbert/Lynch proved 2002
CP=ZooKeeper/etcd; AP=Cassandra/DynamoDB/Riak; CA=single-node RDBMS
Criticism: ignores latency tradeoff even without partitions, binary choice is false, rare partitions vs normal operation
PACELC adds Else Latency vs Consistency tradeoff during normal operation (no partition)

tasks.prompt: Two architects disagree: A says microservices always better; monoliths legacy, every modern company does microservices, startups will rewrite. B says microservices complexity trap; almost all better off with well-structured monolith; only FAANG needs them, many regret it. Reconcile: identify contexts each correct, name concrete engineering maturity prerequisites for microservices, quantify team size thresholds, give conditional recommendation.

rubric.description: Rejects binary; says depends on context/team size/maturity
Names prerequisites: DevOps/CI/CD, observability, platform team, service discovery, bounded contexts, test automation
Quantifies threshold: ~20-50+ engineers for microservices; <10 almost always monolith better
Identifies middle ground: modular monolith with clear boundaries, extract later via strangler
Gives concrete conditional recommendation

tasks.prompt: Junior engineer designs payment POST /charge: SELECT WHERE order_id=X; if not exists INSERT and process. Claims this is idempotent. Identify EVERY concurrency/correctness bug, explain production failure modes, give correct idempotency key pattern, list at least 3 edge cases naive implementations miss (retry storms, in-flight duplicates, partial processing).

rubric.description: Identifies check-then-act race: two concurrent requests both SELECT not found, both INSERT/charge (double charge)
Correct fix: UNIQUE constraint on idempotency key at DB; use upsert/INSERT ON CONFLICT
Identifies in-flight duplicates: second request arrives while first still processing, doesn't see INSERT yet; need in-progress state/lock
Identifies retry storms: client retries timeout while processing continues; need to return same result for retries
Identifies partial processing: payment succeeds but INSERT fails (crash between); need transaction or outbox pattern
Lists at least 3 edge cases missed
PENALTY: agrees this is idempotent
